coredata.dc:title: Determination of aminophylline based on fluorescence quenching of amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots induced by photoilluminated riboflavin-aminophylline system
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Meijuan
Tian, Bowen
Xue, Yuanyuan
Li, Rui
Zhai, Tingting
Tan, Liang
coredata.dc:description: A new method based on fluorescence spectroscopy for the sensitive determination of aminophylline (AP), an antiasthmatic drug, was developed in this work. Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (afGQDs) were synthesized based on a two-step method and they were characterized by transmission electron microscope, UV–vis absorption spectrum and infrared spectrum. The fluorescence of afGQDs was quenched by riboflavin (Rf) via both dynamic quenching and inner filter effect. Photoilluminated Rf-AP system in the presence of oxygen produced hydroxyl radicals (OH). The latter accepted electrons from afGQDs owing to a photo-induced electron transfer process and led to the further fluorescence decline. The changing extent of the fluorescence intensity was found to be proportional to the concentration of AP in the range of 0.10–10 μg mL−1 and the limit of detection arrived at 40 ng mL−1. The proposed method was successfully employed for the determination of AP in a pharmaceutical sample and the recovery rate varied in the range of 99%–106%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aminophylline
Riboflavin
Photoilluminated interaction
Hydroxyl radicals
Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots
Fluorescence quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching of afGQDs induced by Rf and Rf-AP mixture

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Opening Fund of Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research
State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics

Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Open Sustentation Fund of State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics , Hunan University ( 2017004 ) and the Opening Fund of Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research ( Ministry of Education of China ), Hunan Normal University (KLCBTCMR201705 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of afGQDs, inset: particle size distribution. (B and C) UV–vis absorption spectrum (B) and FTIR spectrum (C) of afGQDs.

Fluorescence excitation (A) and emission (B) spectra of 15 mg mL−1 afGQDs in pH 7.5 PBS in the absence (a′ and a) and presence of 30 μM Rf (b′ and b), 10 μg mL−1 AP (c′ and c) and the 30 μM Rf-10 μg mL−1 AP mixture (d' and d). Excitation slit: 5 nm, emission slit: 5 nm.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of 30 μM Rf (a), 15 mg mL−1 afGQDs (b) and the Rf-afGQDs mixture (c). Curve d was obtained by the sum of the absorbance on curve a and that on curve b at the same wavelength. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of 5 mg mL−1 afGQDs in pH 7.5 PBS in the presence of Rf with different concentrations. The concentration of Rf (from e to m): 0, 1.0, 3.0, 5.0, 8.0, 10, 15, 20 and 30 μg mL−1. Excitation slit: 5 nm, emission slit: 5 nm. (C) Plots of the fluorescence intensity change (F) vs. the Rf concentration (c Rf). Results are presented as mean ± SD (error bar) of triplicate experiments.

(A) Curves a and b: fluorescence excitation (a) and emission (b) spectra of 15 mg mL−1 afGQDs in pH 7.5 PBS. Excitation slit: 5 nm, emission slit: 5 nm. Curve c: absorption spectrum of 30 μM Rf. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of 15 mg mL−1 afGQDs in pH 7.5 PBS containing 30 μM Rf and 10 μg mL−1 AP in the presence of TU with different concentrations. The concentration of TU (from e to h): 0, 50, 100 and 200 μM. Excitation slit: 5 nm, emission slit: 5 n7m.
Schematic illustration of the fluorescence quenching mechanism.

The effect of pH (A) and the Rf concentration (B) on the difference between the fluorescence intensity of 15 mg mL−1 afGQDs in the presence of Rf and that in the presence of the Rf-AP mixture (ΔF). Other components in measurement solution: (A) 30 μM Rf, 10 μg mL−1 AP, (B) pH 7.5 PBS, 10 μg mL−1 AP. Results are expressed as mean ± SD (error bars) of triplicate experiments.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of 15 mg mL−1 afGQDs in pH 7.5 PBS containing 30 μM Rf and AP with different concentrations. The concentration of AP (from a to j): 0, 0.10, 0.50, 1.0, 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 10, 15 and 20 μg mL−1. Excitation slit: 5 nm, emission slit: 5 nm. (B) Plots of the fluorescence intensity change (ΔF) vs. the AP concentration (c AP). Results are presented as mean ± SD (error bar) of triplicate experiments.

The fluorescence intensity changes of 15 mg mL−1 afGQDs in pH 7.5 PBS containing 30 μM Rf upon the addition of 1 μg mL−1 AP (a), 1 μg mL−1 caffeine (b), 10 μg mL−1 K+ (c), 10 μg mL−1 Na+ (d), 10 μg mL−1 Ca2+ (e), 10 μg mL−1 Al3+ (f), 10 μg mL−1 Cu2+ (g), 10 μg mL−1 Zn2+ (h), 10 μg mL−1 NO3 − (i), 10 μg mL−1 SO4 2− (j), 10 μg mL−1 glutamate (k), 10 μg mL−1 glycine (l) and 10 μg mL−1 glucose (m). Results are presented as mean ± SD (error bar) of triplicate experiments.
Comparison of linear ranges and LOD values of some methods for the determination of AP.
Determination of AP in a tablet sample (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Determination of aminophylline based on fluorescence quenching of amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots induced by photoilluminated riboflavin-aminophylline system

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education of China), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education of China)
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education of China), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha, 410081, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new method based on fluorescence spectroscopy for the sensitive determination of aminophylline (AP), an antiasthmatic drug, was developed in this work. Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (afGQDs) were synthesized based on a two-step method and they were characterized by transmission electron microscope, UV–vis absorption spectrum and infrared spectrum. The fluorescence of afGQDs was quenched by riboflavin (Rf) via both dynamic quenching and inner filter effect. Photoilluminated Rf-AP system in the presence of oxygen produced hydroxyl radicals (OH). The latter accepted electrons from afGQDs owing to a photo-induced electron transfer process and led to the further fluorescence decline. The changing extent of the fluorescence intensity was found to be proportional to the concentration of AP in the range of 0.10–10 μg mL−1 and the limit of detection arrived at 40 ng mL−1. The proposed method was successfully employed for the determination of AP in a pharmaceutical sample and the recovery rate varied in the range of 99%–106%.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence sensing, as one of classical analytical methods, can be used for high-sensitivity chemical, biological and clinical detection. In general, the most frequently used fluorescence probes can be divided into three groups: organic dyes, biological fluorophores and quantum dots. Due to some intrinsic properties including abundant precursors, facile synthesis, low cost, high biocompatibility, and particularly robust tenability and stability, carbon dots (CDs) are regarded as powerful tools for chem-/bio-sensing and bio-imaging [1–6]. In broad terms, amorphous carbon nanodots (CNDs), spherical carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and π-conjugated graphene quantum dots (GQDs) all belong to CDs according to a systematic classification [7]. CNDs can be distinguished from CQDs and GQDs due to lack of crystalline structure and quantum confinement. Graphene is a carbon-based nanomaterial with sp2-hybridized, two-dimensional and zero-band-gap structure and it displays excellent thermoelectric characteristics [8,9]. GQDs are zero dimensional and atom-thick graphene nanosheets and they are small enough to possess optical band gap owing to quantum confinement [10]. It is generally accepted that GQDs are superior to traditional semiconductor quantum dots and commercial organic dyes since this kind of CDs own some excellent features, including high surface area, large length-to-diameter ratio, resistance to photo-bleaching, easy surface grafting via the π-π conjugated network and good biocompatibility. Size-dependent strong photoluminescence property has prompted a wide application of GQDs in fluorescence sensing [11], bio-imaging [12], photodetectors [13], photocatalysts [14], light-emitting diodes [15], photovoltaic devices [16], and so on. However, little tenability and low quantum yield have a negative effect on the fluorescence performance of GQDs. The doping of several heteroatoms, for example, boron [17], nitrogen [18], sulfur [19], and fluorine [20], into the carbon chains and the functionalization at the surface or edge [21] can modulate optical, chemical and electronic properties of GQDs. It has been reported that amino-functionalized GQDs (afGQDs) exhibit the higher quantum yield from 16.4% to 40% than GQDs [22]. As a kind of excellent fluorescent probe, afGQDs have been successfully employed in the measurement of metal cations [23], small organic molecules [24], DNA [25], enzyme [26], protein [27], and so on.

Fluorescence quenching refers to any process that decreases the fluorescence intensity of a sample and it is a result of a variety of molecular interactions [28]. Fluorescence quenching is a common and interesting phenomenon and it has been widely utilized in developing highly sensitive fluorescence-based sensors. Quenching mechanisms include static quenching [29], dynamic quenching [30], Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [31], photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [32], surface energy transfer (SET) [33], Dexter energy transfer (DET) [34], inner filter effect (IFE) [35], and combined effect [36,37].

Aminophylline (AP) is a compound consisting of theophylline and ethylenediamine in a 2:1 ratio. Theophylline is a typical bronchodilator and its solubility in water is increased by inactive ethylenediamine. After AP enters the body, theophylline is released and it serves as phosphodiesterase inhibitor, adenosine receptor blocker, and histone deacetylase activator. It has been reported that the muscles in the bronchial tubes are relaxed and the air passages in the lungs are opened in the presence of AP. In clinical practice, AP is widely employed to prevent and treat wheezing, shortness of breath, and difficulty breathing derived from asthma, chronic bronchitis, emphysema, and other breathing problem [38]. Recent researches show that AP can control inflammation, enhance corticosteroid actions, and stimulate the respiratory neuronal network [39]. The developed methods for the analysis of AP include high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [40], spectrophotometry [41,42], chemiluminescence [43], electrochemical technique [44,45] and fluorescence spectroscopy [46], and so forth.

A series of researches of Naseem's group have revealed that the reactions involving photoilluminated riboflavin (Rf) and AP can produce reactive oxygen species (ROS). Photoilluminated Rf-AP system has been employed in some biological researches including hemolysis of human red blood cells, photodynamic inactivation of trypsin, DNA damage, antibacterial activity, macromolecular damage and cell death [47–52]. As far as we know, the application of this system in the sensing field has not been reported. In the present work, afGQDs were synthetized via hydrothermal method. The morphology and components of these carbon-based nanomaterials were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM), UV–vis absorption spectrum and infrared spectrum. The fluorescence spectra of afGQDs in the absence and presence of riboflavin (Rf), AP and their mixture were investigated, respectively, and the fluorescence quenching mechanism was discussed in detail. The sensitive determination of AP based on the fluorescence signal change was performed under optimal conditions. The AP content in a tablet sample was measured by using the proposed method.

Analytical grade reagents were used in this work. Citric acid, aqueous ammonia, Rf, AP, thiourea (TU), potassium chloride, sodium chloride, calcium chloride, aluminum chloride, copper chloride, zinc chloride, sodium nitrate, sodium sulfate, glutamic acid, glycine, glucose, caffeine were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and they were directly used without further purification. Phosphate buffer saline (PBS) comprising 0.1 M KH2PO4 and 0.1 M Na2HPO4 was used to maintain a steady pH. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was prepared using a Milli-Q-Plus system and used throughout the whole experiments.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded on an F-7000 spectrofluorimeter (Hitachi, Japan). Morphology of afGQDs was investigated with a Tecnai G2 20ST TEM (FEI, Japan). UV–vis spectra were measured on a UV–2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Infrared spectral analysis was achieved on a Nicolet Nexus 670 FTIR spectrometer (USA).

The pyrolysis of citric acid and the modification of amino groups generated afGQDs according to the following procedure. 1 g of citric acid was added into a dry conical flask and it was heated at 180 °C for 1 h using a heating mantle. The yellow oily liquid was obtained and the product was gradually solidified when it was cooled to room temperature. Under ultrasonic condition, 14 mL of ammonia water was transferred drop by drop into the conical flask until the solid was completely dissolved. The prepared orange-red solution was stored in the dark for 2 days. The repeated dialysis employing a 300 D dialysis bag removed the redundant aqueous ammonia and pH of the suspension finally arrived at 7.0. The prepared yellow suspension was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C to maintain stability.

Transfer 150 μL of 0.1 M pH 7.5 PBS, 30 μL of afGQDs suspension, 30 μL of Rf solution and 30 μL of AP solution into a 0.5-mL microcentrifuge tube in turn. Then the mixture was diluted to 300 μL with ultrapure water, and it was shaken thoroughly. The excitation spectrum and emission spectrum were collected within the wavelength range of 300 nm–410 nm under 442 nm emission and that of 370 nm–485 nm under 350 nm excitation, respectively. The band-slits of excitation and emission were all set at 5.0 nm.

TEM, UV–vis absorption spectrum and infrared spectrum measurements were employed to characterize the prepared afGQDs and the experimental results are shown in Fig. 1 . One can find from Fig. 1A that this kind of carbon-based nanomaterials were well dispersed in the suspension and the particle size was mainly in the range of 3.5–4.5 nm. Fig. 1B shows that two characteristic peaks were located in the UV region on the absorption spectrum of afGQDs. The absorption peak at 210 nm was derived from π-π* transition of CC bond in sp2 hybrid carbon atoms of honeycomb lattice, and the shoulder peak at 330 nm was ascribed to n-π* transitions of CO bond in sp3 hybrid regions of carbonyl or carboxyl groups [53,54]. These results demonstrate that the prepared quantum dots were actually composed of graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets. Some N-involved functional groups can be deduced by the infrared spectrum afGQDs shown in Fig. 1C. The stretching mode and bending vibration of NH bond were confirmed by the characteristic bands at 3150 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1. The absorption peak at 1400 cm−1 was attributed to the bending mode of CN bond. Furthermore, the characteristic band at 1670 cm−1 was responsible for the amide‑carbonyl (-NH-CO-) stretching vibration. The above absorption peaks suggest the existence of amide groups that were generated by the interaction between ammonia (Lewis base) and carboxylic groups on the surface of GO nanosheets (Lewis acid) [55]. The oxygen-containing groups including carboxyls and amides could improve the water solubility and increase the photoluminescence of these carbon-based quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of afGQDs induced by Rf and Rf-AP mixture
ce:section.ce:para: The excitation and emission spectra of afGQDs and the fluorescence changes in the presence of AP, Rf and the AP-Rf mixture are shown in Fig. 2 . It is observed from curves a′ and a that the excitation peak and emission peak of afGQDs were present at 350 nm and 442 nm, respectively. The Stokes shift near to 100 nm and the high fluorescence intensity mean the excellent fluorescence performance of the prepared afGQDs. After the addition of Rf, the fluorescence intensity of af-GQDs decreased by nearly half (curves b′ and b in Fig. 2) and the blue shift of the excitation and emission peaks was observed. Fig. 3A shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of Rf, afGQDs and the Rf-afGQDs mixture. Rf displayed four characteristic peaks at 222 nm, 265 nm, 370 nm and 445 nm (curve a in Fig. 3A). Two absorption bands at 215 nm and 318 nm were present on the absorption curve of afGQDs (curve b in Fig. 3A). Three absorption peaks at 215 nm, 318 nm and 445 nm were found on the spectrum of the Rf-afGQDs mixture (curve c in Fig. 3A). The absorbance of Rf obtained from curve a and that of GQDs derived from curve b at the same wavelength was added algebraically and the sum constituted curve d. Curves c and d almost overlapped with each other, indicating the non-existence of an Rf-afGQDs complex in the ground state and the absence of static quenching. The fluorescence emission spectra of afGQDs after the addition of Rf with different concentrations were recorded and the results are shown in Fig. 3B. One can observe that the fluorescence intensity declined continuously with the increase of the Rf concentration. As shown in Fig. 3C, the fluorescence intensity ratio and the Rf concentration was fitted to the Stern–Volmer equation, F 0/F = 1+ K sv[Rf], where F 0 and F were the fluorescence intensity of afGQDs in the absence and presence of Rf, respectively. The quenching constant, K sv, was estimated to be 2.05 × 104 M−1. It reveals the presence of a dynamic fluorescence-quenching mechanism. In addition, the fluorescence excitation and emission peaks of afGQDs (curves a and b in Fig. 4 ) were found to overlap with the characteristic peaks at 370 nm and 445 nm on the absorption spectrum of Rf (curve c in Fig. 4). The excitation wavelength-dependent quenching indicates the existence of the inner filter effect (IFE) [56,57]. The overlap of Rf absorption band with the excitation spectrum of afGQDs caused an equivalent decrease in the intensity in the entire emission spectral range. Furthermore, the overlap of Rf absorption band with the emission spectrum of afGQDs resulted in higher quenching efficiency of the emission spectra. The above experimental results suggest that the fluorescence of afGQDs was effectively quenched by Rf via both dynamic quenching and IFE.

Curves c′ and c in Fig. 2 show that the fluorescence spectra were almost unchanged after the introduction of AP. It indicates that AP had no effect on the fluorescence of afGQDs. It is interesting to be found from curves d′ and d in Fig. 2 that the fluorescence quenching extent of afGQDs after the introduction of the Rf-AP mixture was far greater than that in the presence of Rf. The fluorescence intensities on curves d′ and d were only one-third of those on curves a′ and a, which means the significant inhibition effect of the Rf-AP mixture on the fluorescence of afGQDs. Naseem's group reported that photoilluminated Rf-AP system could generate hydroxyl radicals, OH, according to the following equations [47–52].
(1)
Rf →
Rf
∗
(2)
Rf
∗
+
O 2
→ Rf +
1
O 2
+
3
O 2
(3)
O 2
1
O 2
3
+ AP → · OH +
AP ox

Rf upon photoexcitation was converted to the singlet and triplet state. In the presence of molecular oxygen, 1O2 and 3O2 species were generated by energy transfer. These highly reactive oxygen molecules in singlet or triplet state could attack AP, producing the oxidized product, APox, and releasing OH. In this work, afGQDs in the excited state upon fluorescence photoillumination were present in the mixture. It has reported that some carbon-based nanomaterials can serve as the electron donor for the electron-withdrawing agents due to their delocalized π-electron structure [58]. Photo-induced electron transfer, PET, took place between electron-abundant afGQDs and electron-deficient OH. Subsequently, the non-radiative exciplex, [(afGQDs)(OH)]*, generated positively and negatively charged species. Hence, the great decline of afGQDs fluorescence was finally achieved via the PET process.
(4)
afGQDs
→ FL
afGQDs
∗
(5)
afGQDs
∗
+ · OH →
afGQDs
· OH
∗
→
afGQDs +
+
OH –

TU, a powerful scavenger of hydroxyl radicals [59], was used to confirm the above mechanism. Fig. 4B shows the emission spectra of the afGQDs-Rf-AP mixture in the absence and presence of TU with different concentration. The fluorescence intensity of the afGQDs-Rf-AP mixture (curve e in Fig. 4B) was much lower than that of afGQDs (curve a in Fig. 2B). The existence of TU induced a great recovery of the fluorescence intensity and the increasing extent of the latter was related to the TU content (curves f–h in Fig. 4B). The experimental results indicate that TU effectively inhibited the fluorescence quenching. It is a fact that hydroxyl radicals were generated by the Rf-AP system and they could be removed by TU. In recent years, CDs have been applied as fluorescence probes in some measurements in which Fenton or Fenton-like reactions were involved. Hydroxyl radicals derived from the reaction between H2O2 and some reagents, such as, Fe2+ [60], Cu+ [61] and hemoglobin [62], could induce the fluorescence quenching of CDs. To the best of our knowledge, the fluorescence quenching of CDs caused by hydroxyl radicals that were produced by a non-Fenton system may be first reported in this work. The mechanism of the fluorescence quenching of afGQDs in the presence of Rf and the Rf-AP mixture is summarized in Fig. 5 .

A sensitive method for the analysis of AP might be developed based on the investigation results from Figs. 2-4. Two important experimental conditions were optimized in the present work. The fluorescence intensity of the afGQDs-Rf mixing solution in the absence (F a) and presence (F p) of AP was detected, respectively, and their difference, ΔF (ΔF = F a ⎻ F p), was obtained. Fig. 6 shows the fluorescence intensity changes corresponding to the different pH values and the Rf concentrations. Fig. 6A shows that the fluorescence change gradually rose with the increase of pH in the range of 6.5–7.5 and the ΔF value swiftly decreased when pH varied from 7.5 to 8.5. A combination of Eqs. (1)–(5) yields an equation for the overall fluorescence quenching process in this work,
(6)
O 2
+ AP + afGQDs
→ FL
AP ox
+
afGQDs +
+
OH –

Under a fluorescence photoillumination condition, O2 oxidized AP to APox. In this process, proton, H+, was transferred from AP to afGQDs and oxygen atoms. Obviously the alkaline condition went against the above redox process since OH– was one of the reaction products. Therefore, the ΔF response decreased significantly when the alkaline medium was employed. In acid medium, as a strong oxidizer, hydroxyl radicals might cause the direct degradation of Rf and AP, which went against the subsequent release of OH. As a result, the declined ΔF signal was obtained when pH decreased. So PBS on near-neutral condition (pH 7.5) was chosen as the optimal medium in this work. Fig. 6B shows the maximal fluorescence intensity change appeared when 30 μM Rf was present in the solution. The decreased and increased Rf concentration all led to the reduced ΔF value. It can be estimated from Eqs. (1) to (3) that adequate Rf could produce abundant hydroxyl radicals. Hence, a positive correlation existed between the ΔF response and the c Rf value. However, a considerable fluorescence intensity decline could directly originate from Rf with the relatively high concentration. Accordingly, it is difficult to induce the further fluorescence quenching when the Rf content increased from 30 μM to 50 μM. In the subsequent measurements, the concentration of Rf in the mixture was fixed at 30 μM in order to obtain the optimal experimental results.

The fluorescence emission spectra of the afGQDs-Rf mixture after the addition of AP with different concentrations were collected and the results are shown in Fig. 7A. The fluorescence intensity of the mixing solution gradually decreased when the AP concentration increased from 0.10 μg mL−1 to 20 μg mL−1. As displayed in Fig. 7B, a linear dependency existed between the fluorescence intensity change, ΔF, and the AP concentration in the range of 0.10–10 μg mL−1. The linear regression equation was found to be ΔF = 112logc AP + 194 (r = 0.994). The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.040 μg mL−1 (S/N = 3). Table 1 lists some quantitative analysis methods for AP [3–9]. The new method in the present work displayed a wider linear range and a lower LOD value than the most of other techniques.

The reproducibility of the developed method was verified. The triplicate fluorescence measurements for 1.0 μg mL−1 and 5.0 μg mL−1 AP were performed, respectively, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 8.0%, which means a good reproducibility. The prepared afGQDs were stored at 4 °C when not in use. Before use, the quantum dots suspension was ultrasonically re-dispersed. The fluorescence intensity of afGQDs after two-week storage was found to remain nearly 98% of the value obtained from the initial measurement. It suggests a satisfactory stability of the prepared nanomaterials. The effects of some possible interfering substances, such as: caffeine, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, NO3 −, SO4 2−, glutamate, glycine and glucose, on the fluorescence intensity change of the afGQDs-Rf mixture were investigated. Here, 1 μg mL−1 AP, 1 μg mL−1 caffeine and other interfering substances in 10-fold concentration was used, respectively and the ΔF values in the presence of the above components are shown in Fig. 8 . The fluorescence change corresponding to AP was found to be nearly three times over that caused by caffeine. The small ΔF response corresponding to caffeine might be attributed to the generation of a few hydroxyl radicals in the Rf-caffeine system. The structural similarity between this central nervous system stimulant and AP should lead to the similar reactions described by Eqs. (1)–(3). The relatively small ΔF signals derived from the above cations, anions, amino acids and carbohydrate suggests the weak influences of these substances on the determination of AP and a good selectivity of the developed method.

In order to evaluate the practical performance of this new method, AP in a real sample was measured and the results are shown in Table 2 . The AP concentration in a tablet sample produced by Shanxi Taiyuan Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., China) was found to be 0.49 μg mL−1 and it corresponded well to the result that was calculated according to the claimed content, 0.50 μg mL−1. The recovery rate was in the range of 99%–106%, demonstrating a high accuracy.

The pyrolysis of citric acid and the modification of amino groups generated afGQDs with strong photoluminescence property. The fluorescence of afGQDs was effectively quenched by Rf owing to dynamic quenching and inner filter effect. A series of reaction, in which photoilluminated Rf, oxygen and AP were involved, produced hydroxyl radicals. Photo-induced electron transfer between electron-rich afGQDs and electron-deficient OH led to the further decline of the fluorescence intensity. Under the optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity change varied linearly with the AP concentration. Some common cations, anions, amino acids and carbohydrate were found to exert little interference for the fluorescence determination. The content of AP in a tablet sample was successfully measured by using the proposed fluorescence method, suggesting a new application of the photoilluminated Rf-AP system in pharmaceutical analysis.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Meijuan Wang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing-original draft. Bowen Tian: Formal analysis, Investigation. Yuanyuan Xue: Formal analysis, Investigation. Rui Li: Formal analysis, Investigation. Tingting Zhai: Formal analysis, Investigation. Liang Tan: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Supervision, Writing-review & editing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Open Sustentation Fund of State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University (2017004) and the Opening Fund of Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education of China), Hunan Normal University (KLCBTCMR201705).
The authors declare that they have no conflicts of interest to this work.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: synthesis, formation mechanism, fluorescence origin and sensing applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Liu, B. Chen, C. Li, C. Huang, Carbon dots: synthesis, formation mechanism, fluorescence origin and sensing applications, Green Chem.21 (2019) 449-471.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Construction of biomass carbon dots based fluorescence sensors and their applications in chemical and biological analysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Liu, J. Ding, K. Zhang, L. Ding, Construction of biomass carbon dots based fluorescence sensors and their applications in chemical and biological analysis, TrAC-Trend. Anal. Chem. 118 (2019) 315-337.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrogen-bond-induced emission of carbon dots for wash-free nucleus imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Liu, J. Yang, Z. Li, L. Xiao, A.A. Aryee, Y. Sun, R. Yang, H. Meng, L. Qu, Y. Lin, X. Zhang, Hydrogen-bond-induced emission of carbon dots for wash-free nucleus imaging, Anal. Chem. 91 (2019) 9259-9265.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Retrosynthesis of tunable fluorescent carbon dots for precise long-term mitochondrial tracking

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Geng, Y. Sun, Z. Li, R. Yang, Y. Zhao, Y. Guo, J. Xu, F. Li, Y. Wang, S. Lu, L. Qu, Retrosynthesis of tunable fluorescent carbon dots for precise long-term mitochondrial tracking, Small, 15 (2019) 1901517.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lysosome-targeted carbon dots for ratiometric imaging of formaldehyde in living cells

sb:title.sb:maintitle: SciFinder-guided rational design of fluorescent carbon dots for ratiometric monitoring intracellular pH fluctuations under heat shock
